comments.text: Let \(A\subset\mathbb N\) be an asymptotic additive basis of order \(k\), and suppose that \(A\) is strongly minimal under infinite deletions in the sense that, for every infinite \(B\subset A\), the set \(A\setminus B\) is not an asymptotic basis of order \(k\). Must there exist an infinite \(B\subset A\) such that \(A\setminus B\) is an asymptotic basis of order \(k+1\)? We give a complete answer. For \(k=1\), the answer is yes. For every \(k\ge2\), the answer is no. More strongly, for every \(k\ge2\), we construct a set \(A\subset\mathbb N\) such that \(A\) is an asymptotic basis of order \(k\), is ordinarily minimal at order \(k\), is strongly minimal under infinite deletions at order \(k\), and satisfies the stronger property that \(A\setminus B\) is not an asymptotic basis of order \(k+1\) for every infinite \(B\subset A\). The examples may be chosen thin, with\[ A(x) = \lvert A \cap [0, x] \rvert = O_k(x^{1/k}) \]which is optimal up to constants for bases of order \(k\). Final write up here: https://www.overleaf.com/read/dckvqtggbjzn#9b7dd7 Main Solution Thread with overleaf generation https://chatgpt.com/share/69f6a9c4-9978-83ea-a2a5-d18a03f6a01d Main Referee Thread https://www.perplexity.ai/search/ff1df21a-865b-4cd3-a494-98abaa7958b7#23
Please clearly disclose how AI was used when finding proofs; in this case, for example, it seems the proof was entirely generated by GPT.
Standard check claimed several major gaps in the writeup in the current form.
